text.title: Client Conventions As Code Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Client Conventions As Code application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: `conventions.yaml` precedence is hard-coded: client overrides > faion defaults > engine defaults. AI agents and lint runners MUST load in this order; any deviation rejects the engagement-level config.
rule.rationale: Without explicit precedence, conflicting rules silently win by load order; pinning the order prevents 'faion told the agent to do X when the client said Y' incidents.

rule.statement: Every entry in `conventions.yaml` MUST carry a `source` field pointing to the client artefact (style guide section, ADR, email, ticket). Un-sourced rules are rejected at validation.
rule.rationale: Un-sourced rules accumulate as folklore and the client cannot audit them; sourcing keeps the file falsifiable.

rule.statement: The file MUST contain a `signoff` block: client owner name + email + date + commit SHA. Without signoff the file is treated as draft and AI agents fall back to faion defaults.
rule.rationale: Unsigned conventions become 'someone said' and drift; the signoff is the renewal anchor.

rule.statement: Every lint config, AI-agent rule, or pre-commit hook generated FROM `conventions.yaml` MUST embed a header comment with the file path + commit SHA of the source. Manually-edited generated files are rejected on next regenerate.
rule.rationale: Generated files drift when humans patch them in place; the trace makes drift visible and one-step recoverable.

rule.statement: Any addition / removal / modification of a rule MUST go through a PR review with the client signoff owner as a required reviewer. Direct commits to `conventions.yaml` on main are rejected at branch protection.
rule.rationale: The file is governance, not code; same review discipline as IAM policy.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
